summary: McKenzie argues that Silicon Valley's diversity problem is really a symptom of deeper structural pathologies: the startup model itself is a terrible value proposition for most intelligent people regardless of gender. Rather than focusing on demographic headcounts, the Valley should fix its broken fundamentals — overwork culture, VC dependency, geographic concentration, and building products nobody pays for. These fixes would naturally make startups more attractive to a broader population, including women, while also producing better businesses.
key_points: Demographic diversity is a lazy proxy for diversity of thought — measuring checkbox demographics instead of actual cognitive diversity is like counting hits instead of conversions

The startup value proposition is objectively terrible: 100-hour weeks, poverty wages, geographic lock-in to an expensive area, and 90%+ failure rate — the real question is why anyone accepts this deal
The Valley creates a persistent undersupply of products for women and other underserved markets, which represents enormous untapped business opportunity
The diversity discourse wants more women but specifically more women who conform to existing Valley culture — equity-compensated, coding until 2 AM, building flip-to-Google companies
Practical fixes include bootstrapping over VC funding, building outside the Valley, making products people pay for, and normalizing humane working hours
money_quotes: True diversity, of course, is the 5-member iStockPhoto of attractive twenty-somethings sitting on the college quad who check different boxes on the demographic inventory and think alike in every way that matters.

We sound like we're counting hits because grepping the Apache log is easy and implementing conversion funnel tracking is hard, despite the fact that we know hits are meaningless and we're really interested in conversions.
My beef with the discourse of 'diversity' in a nutshell: it screams 'give us more women' and whispers 'give us more women like us'.
The question isn't what are we doing that's keeping ladies out of the Valley, gentlemen. The question should be why in God's name are we still here.
Unsurprisingly, the conversion rate for the above sales pitch has lagged expectations.
Persistent undersupply is one of those words that should be music to your ears if you are a capitalist, because market failures are opportunities to make lots and lots of money.
I checked my Ruby standard library: there is no TriedToHaveALifeOutsideOfWorkException.

After you're profitable, if you really want to, you can go to the Valley. My guess is they'll fall over themselves trying to give you money because they have no freaking clue how to create success in a reproducible fashion.
key_insight: Silicon Valley's gender imbalance is a symptom of a fundamentally broken startup model that rational people of any gender should reject — fix the model and the demographics will follow.
